items.title: [compiler-v2] Fix type inference to allow unifying struct constraints and receiver function constraints
[compiler-v2] Allow receiver functions on fully concrete struct instantiations
items.body:
## Description

Previously, Move receiver functions (dot-call syntax with self) on generic structs required a fully generic identity instantiation — every type argument had to be a distinct type parameter (e.g., `fun foo(self: S<T, R>)`). This blocked patterns like `fun foo(self: S<u64, bool>)`.

This PR relaxes the requirement to also accept fully concrete instantiations where all type arguments are closed types with no type parameters anywhere in them.

What's allowed now:
  - `fun get_x(self: &MyStruct<u64, bool>): u64` — all type args are concrete types

What's still rejected:
  - Partial instantiation (`G<u64, T>`) — mix of concrete and type params
  - Non-linear type params (`G<T, T>`) — duplicate type parameters
  - Nested generics as type args (`G<vector<T>, T>`) — open but not plain type parameters

## How Has This Been Tested?
- new tests added to show both positive and negative cases

## Type of Change
- [x] New feature

## Which Components or Systems Does This Change Impact?
- [x] Move Compiler

<!-- CURSOR_SUMMARY -->
---

> [!NOTE]
> **Medium Risk**
> Adjusts compiler receiver-function registration rules for generic structs, which can change which functions are eligible for dot-call dispatch and may affect diagnostics/backward compatibility at compile time.
> 
> **Overview**
> Enables Move receiver (dot-call) functions to be declared on *fully concrete* instantiations of generic structs (e.g. `fun f(self: S<u64, bool>)`), in addition to the existing fully-generic “all distinct type parameters” form.
> 
> Updates the receiver validation logic in `move-model` to reject only *mixed open/closed* (partial) instantiations and keeps rejecting non-linear and nested-open generic arguments, and adds new positive/negative compiler + transactional tests covering concrete receivers, chaining (including through vector indexing), and cross-module behavior with updated diagnostics expectations.
> 
> <sup>Written by [Cursor Bugbot](https://cursor.c

items.title: [consensus] Remove randomness fast path code
items.body:
## Summary

- Remove all randomness fast path runtime code (ConfigV2, PathType, FastShare, fast_rand_config, broadcast_fast_shares, etc.) across ~29 files, net deletion of ~800 lines
- The fast path used a separate, higher secrecy threshold for faster randomness reconstruction but was disabled on mainnet and is being deprecated
- Preserve backward compatibility with BCS-serialized on-chain and local DB state by retaining `Transcripts.fast` and `AugmentedData.fast_delta` Option fields (always `None`)
- Keep Move `ConfigV2` struct (published on-chain type) but remove `new_v2()` so no new V2 configs can be created
- Handle old key pair serialization format gracefully in epoch manager (fall through to regenerate)
- Delete redundant `e2e_correctness_without_fast_path` smoke test (duplicate of `e2e_correctness`)
- Add `test_key_pair_serialization_backward_compat` unit test to verify DB format migration

## Test plan

- [x] `cargo check` passes for aptos-types, aptos-consensus, aptos-dkg, aptos-release-builder, smoke-test
- [x] `cargo test -p aptos-types --lib` — 213 passed
- [x] `cargo test -p aptos-consensus --lib` — 316 passed (2 pre-existing flaky failures)
- [x] `cargo test -p aptos-dkg` — all passed
- [ ] CI should confirm no regressions in smoke tests

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

<!-- CURSOR_SUMMARY -->
---

> [!NOTE]
> **Medium Risk**
> Touches consensus randomness and DKG/serialization paths; although behavior is simplified, regressions could impact randomness generation/verification or epoch startup if compatibility handling is incorrect.
> 
> **Overview**
> Removes the *randomness fast path* end-to-end across consensus, networking, and DKG: deletes `FastShare`/`PathType` flows, fast-share broadcasting, dual-config aggregation logic, and related logging/tracing.
> 
> Randomness generation is simplified to a single `RandConfig` path (including share aggregation and augmented data verification), while keeping BCS/backward compati

items.title: [storage] Remove dead StateValueSchema and legacy monolithic ledger DB support
items.body:
`StateValueSchema` and its column family `"state_value"` are no longer used
by any production code path. All state value reads/writes go through
`StateValueByKeyHashSchema` via the sharded `StateKvDb`. The monolithic
`LedgerDb` has been fully decomposed into individual sub-databases, so
`ledger_db_column_families()` and `gen_ledger_cfds()` were unreachable.

Removed:
- `schema/state_value/` module (definition + tests)
- `STATE_VALUE_CF_NAME` constant
- `ledger_db_column_families()` and `gen_ledger_cfds()`
- Dead `LEDGER_DB_NAME` match arm in `gen_cfds_by_name()`
- Duplicate `LEDGER_DB_NAME` constant in `ledger_db/mod.rs` (kept in `common.rs`)

<!-- CURSOR_SUMMARY -->
---

> [!NOTE]
> **Medium Risk**
> Storage schema/CF list changes could break opening or migrating nodes that still have the legacy `state_value`/monolithic ledger DB layout on disk, despite being described as unreachable in production.
> 
> **Overview**
> Removes the unused `StateValueSchema` and drops the legacy `"state_value"` column family constant and fuzz/test coverage.
> 
> Cleans up legacy monolithic ledger DB support by deleting `ledger_db_column_families()`/`gen_ledger_cfds()`, removing the `LEDGER_DB_NAME` constant from `ledger_db/mod.rs`, and deleting the corresponding `gen_cfds_by_name()` match arm; state-key prefix extraction is now only configured for the key-hash state value CFs.
> 
> <sup>Written by [Cursor Bugbot](https://cursor.com/dashboard?tab=bugbot) for commit 922677d2163407634c5e0123c59a7831617fd8ad. This will update automatically on new commits. Configure [here](https://cursor.com/dashboard?tab=bugbot).</sup>
<!-- /CURSOR_SUMMARY -->

items.title: Deploy multiple PFNs via helm releases
items.body:
## Summary
- Remove old direct-install PFN code (`install_public_fullnode`, `fullnode.rs`) that created PFNs as individual K8s resources
- Replace `num_pfns` config key with `pfn-deployments` array, where each entry specifies a `helmReleaseName` for a separate helm release
- Add shared PFN node config via `with_pfn_override_node_config_fn()` injected into `pfn-values.fullnode.config`
- Add per-PFN node config overrides via `PfnDeployment` struct and `with_pfn_deployments()`, injected into each `pfn-deployments[i].values.fullnode.config` (deep-merged on top of shared base by the deployer)
- Bump forge deployer image to `82fd1a3d48c698ff9ecef8ab4f9c5141fc94d22a`
- Set default to `with_num_pfns(2)` in realistic env test

### New config API

**Simple usage** (N identical PFNs):
```rust
config.with_num_pfns(2)  // creates pfn-0, pfn-1 with no per-PFN overrides
```

**Shared base config** (applied to all PFNs via `pfn-values`):
```rust
config.with_pfn_override_node_config_fn(Arc::new(|config, _base| {
    // shared config for all PFNs
}))
```

**Per-PFN overrides** (deep-merged on top of shared base):
```rust
config.with_pfn_deployments(vec![
    PfnDeployment { name: "pfn-0".into(), node_config_override_fn: None },
    PfnDeployment {
        name: "pfn-1".into(),
        node_config_override_fn: Some(Arc::new(|config, _base| {
            // per-PFN override for pfn-1 only
        })),
    },
])
```

Depends on https://github.com/aptos-labs/internal-ops/pull/7353

## Test plan
- [x] `cargo check -p aptos-forge -p aptos-forge-cli` passes
- [ ] E2E: deploy forge with `.with_num_pfns(2)` and verify 2 PFNs are created as separate helm releases

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

<!-- CURSOR_SUMMARY -->
---

> [!NOTE]
> **Medium Risk**
> Changes how PFNs are deployed/configured in the k8s backend and removes the old direct-install path, so mis-specified deployer values could break PFN bring-up. No authentication or on-chain logic is modified.
>

items.title: [storage] Clean up all #[allow(dead_code)] under storage/
items.body:
Replace blanket `#[allow(dead_code)]` suppressions with proper fixes:

- Gate test-only code with `#[cfg(test)]` or `#[cfg(any(test, feature = "fuzzing"))]`
- Gate `get_usage_before_or_at` with `#[cfg(feature = "db-debugger")]` matching its only caller
- Remove genuinely dead code: `get_actual_target_version`, `get_committed_version`,
  `get_in_progress_state_kv_snapshot_version`, `get_column_families_by_name`
- Remove stale module-level `#![allow(dead_code)]` from `truncation_helper.rs` and
  `ledger_db/mod.rs`
- Clean up imports that became unused as a result

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

<!-- CURSOR_SUMMARY -->
---

> [!NOTE]
> **Low Risk**
> Mostly compile-time cleanup (removing unused helpers and narrowing visibility via `cfg`), with minimal runtime impact; risk is limited to potential build/feature-flag mismatches.
> 
> **Overview**
> Removes blanket `#[allow(dead_code)]` usage under `storage/` by deleting genuinely unused helpers (e.g. ledger metadata/version and restore/ledger DB utility functions) and tightening module APIs.
> 
> Test/fuzz-only code paths are now explicitly gated with `#[cfg(test)]` or `#[cfg(any(test, feature = "fuzzing"))]` (e.g. pruner pending checks, hot-state merge tracking, transaction auxiliary data helpers), and `LedgerMetadataDb::get_usage_before_or_at` is gated behind `db-debugger` to match its only caller. Imports and minor structure are adjusted accordingly to keep non-test builds warning-free.
> 
> <sup>Written by [Cursor Bugbot](https://cursor.com/dashboard?tab=bugbot) for commit 2c65e70825af01520e8fd9c4cf3578bb439262fd. This will update automatically on new commits. Configure [here](https://cursor.com/dashboard?tab=bugbot).</sup>
<!-- /CURSOR_SUMMARY -->

items.title: [move flow] adding supports for unit test generation
items.body:
## Description

This PR introduces test generation into move-flow. 

It adds two tools into the MCP server: 
- `move_package_test` — Run Move unit tests for a package. With `establish_baseline=true`, saves current coverage as a reference point. On subsequent runs, reports which lines became newly covered since the baseline.
- `move_package_coverage` — Return uncovered source line numbers per file for a package. Runs tests first if no coverage data exists. Used to identify code paths that
   lack test coverage.

It introduces the `mote-test` skill, which encodes a workflow using the MCP tools to autamte test case generation
- aims to generate tests for non-covered code paths
- failed tests are further inspected to distinguish bad tests and real bugs
- support both full-scale-mode and specific-target mode

It also does a few other things:
- fix a bug in `SourceCoverageBuilder`(under `third_party`) that skipped modules with no coverage data, causing all lines to appear as `+` when used for coverage analysis. 
- enable `--version` subcommand in `move-flow`
- fix MCP tools to return full error messages instead of only the outermost context.
- add a `move_package.md` to help with fixing unresoved named addresses
- add `test_mode` and `verify_mode` parameters to `build_model` at the Aptos framework level, propagated through `BuildConfig`. 
    - `test_mode=false, verify_mode=true` for use in `prover.rs`
    -  `test_mode=true, verify_mode=false` for use in MCP
- re-org commonly used small helpers into `utilities`

Note: summary of impacts of adding `test_mode` and `verify_mode` parameters to `build_model` at `aptos-move/framework/src/built_package.rs` (to support this change, a few lower level functions under `third_party` were also changed with their impacts considered). 

| # | Caller | Source | Via | Before (`compile_test`, `compile_verify`) | After (`compile_test`, `compile_verify`) | Changed? |                          
  |---|--------|--------|-----|-----------------

items.title: [Network] Add priority inbound peer support and eviction.
items.body:
## Description
This PR adds priority inbound peer support to the network configs. Specifically, it allows nodes to specify priority inbound peers that should always be allowed to connect, even when the inbound connection limit has been reached. 

When a priority peer attempts to connect and all slots are full, the system will randomly evict a non-priority peer to make room. Note: if all peers are priority peers, then no connections will be evicted, and the new connection attempt will fail.

## Testing Plan
New and existing test infrastructure.


<!-- CURSOR_SUMMARY -->
---

> [!NOTE]
> **Medium Risk**
> Changes core inbound connection admission logic in `PeerManager` by adding eviction behavior, which could affect network stability and peer churn if misconfigured or if eviction edge cases occur.
> 
> **Overview**
> Adds `NetworkConfig.priority_inbound_peers` and plumbs it through `NetworkBuilder`/`PeerManagerBuilder` into `PeerManager`.
> 
> When the unknown inbound connection limit is exceeded, inbound connections from configured *priority peers* are now accepted by randomly evicting an existing inbound, `PeerRole::Unknown`, non-priority peer; if no evictable peer exists, the priority connection is rejected as before. This also refactors disconnect handling into a shared internal path and introduces a new `aptos_priority_peer_evictions` counter plus targeted unit tests covering accept/evict/reject and eviction randomness/cleanup.
> 
> <sup>Written by [Cursor Bugbot](https://cursor.com/dashboard?tab=bugbot) for commit 6b23c768471979cb1a97bcbc1b81ef2b12a42bb1. This will update automatically on new commits. Configure [here](https://cursor.com/dashboard?tab=bugbot).</sup>
<!-- /CURSOR_SUMMARY -->

items.title: separating move harness, so it can be included without feature="testing"
items.body:

e2e-move-tests hardcodes feature="testing".
I assume it was not meant to be included, but Etna rust code included it. 
Which made the whole rust workspace (including production services) to run with feature="testing", which is undesirable. We had to hardcode gas and unit prices etc.

items.title: fix(wallet-adapter-react): use React JSX intrinsic types in headless component utils
items.body:
## Summary

  This PR fixes JSX intrinsic element typing in `@aptos-labs/wallet-adapter-react` by importing `JSX` and `ClassAttributes` directly from
  React and replacing global `JSX.IntrinsicElements` references with `ReactJSX.IntrinsicElements`.

  ## Why

  In some TypeScript/React setups, the global `JSX` namespace may resolve inconsistently and cause type errors for consumers. Using React-
  scoped JSX types improves compatibility and stability.

  ## Changes

  - Updated `packages/wallet-adapter-react/src/components/utils.tsx`
    - `import type { ClassAttributes, JSX as ReactJSX, ReactNode } from "react"`
    - Replaced:
      - `keyof JSX.IntrinsicElements` -> `keyof ReactJSX.IntrinsicElements`
      - `JSX.IntrinsicElements[...]` -> `ReactJSX.IntrinsicElements[...]`
      - `React.ClassAttributes` -> `ClassAttributes`
  - Added a patch changeset:
    - `.changeset/cold-planes-share.md`

  ## Validation

  - `pnpm --filter @aptos-labs/wallet-adapter-core build`
  - `pnpm --filter @aptos-labs/wallet-adapter-react build:declarations`

  Both commands completed successfully.

  ## Impact

  - Type-only change in `wallet-adapter-react`
  - No runtime behavior changes expected

  Closes #781

<!-- CURSOR_SUMMARY -->
---

> [!NOTE]
> **Low Risk**
> Low risk: type-only changes to `createHeadlessComponent` generics plus a patch changeset; no runtime logic is modified.
> 
> **Overview**
> Improves TypeScript compatibility in `wallet-adapter-react` by **replacing global `JSX.IntrinsicElements` usage with React-scoped `React.JSX`/`ClassAttributes` imports** in the headless component utility, avoiding namespace resolution issues in some TS/React setups.
> 
> Adds a **patch changeset** to publish the typing fix.
>

items.title: fix(derived-wallets): unregister old derived wallets on re-announcement
items.body:
## Summary

- Fixes a bug where `setupAutomaticDerivation` in all three derived wallet packages (Ethereum, Solana, Sui) did not unregister previously registered derived wallets when a provider re-announced with the same identifier
- The old unregister callback stored in the `registrations` map was silently overwritten, leaving stale wallets in the wallet-standard registry
- Added a single line to each `deriveAndRegisterWallet` function to call the existing unregister callback before overwriting

Fixes #780

## Test plan

- [ ] Verify WalletConnect disconnect → reconnect no longer leaves stale Ethereum derived wallets in the registry
- [ ] Verify `connect(walletName)` finds the fresh derived wallet after provider re-announcement
- [ ] Confirm no regressions for wallets that only register once (the `?.()` is a no-op when no prior registration exists)


Made with [Cursor](https://cursor.com)

<!-- CURSOR_SUMMARY -->
---

> [!NOTE]
> **Low Risk**
> Low-risk bugfix that only adjusts wallet registration lifecycle by invoking the previous unregister callback before overwriting it. Main risk is unintended wallet disappearance if a provider re-announces unexpectedly, but the change is minimal and guarded with optional calls.
> 
> **Overview**
> Prevents stale derived wallets from lingering in the wallet-standard registry by **unregistering any existing derived wallet** (via the stored unsubscribe callback) before registering a replacement when the same provider/wallet is re-announced.
> 
> Applies this fix consistently across the Ethereum (`detail.info.rdns`), Solana (`wallet.name`), and Sui (`wallet.name`) automatic-derivation flows, and publishes patch releases via a changeset.
> 
> <sup>Written by [Cursor Bugbot](https://cursor.com/dashboard?tab=bugbot) for commit 06fc3bf0102176d7a5051b256c6565c35c7a00b9. This will update automatically on new commits. Configure [here](https://cursor.com/dashboard?tab=bugbot).</sup>
<!-- /CURSOR_SUMMARY -->

items.title: feat: add CLI flags for non-interactive and hybrid scaffolding
items.body:
## Summary

Make create-aptos-dapp AI-friendly. Currently, AI cannot scaffold a template due to its interactive design. Adding robust flags to let AI scaffold templates with a single cli command. 

I've taken the hybrid scaffolding approach, letting users add partial flags, and the interactive session will only prompt the user for the remaining choices. 

- Add Commander.js CLI flags (`--project-type/-p`, `--template/-t`, `--framework/-f`, `--network/-n`, `--use-surf`, `--api-key`, `--name`, `[project-name]` positional) that map 1:1 to each interactive prompt, enabling AI agents and CI pipelines to scaffold projects without interactive prompts
- When all required flags are present, run fully non-interactively; when only some are provided, prompt for the rest (hybrid mode); when none are provided, existing interactive flow is unchanged
- Add `--list` flag that outputs structured JSON of all valid options and constraints for programmatic discovery
- Validate flag combinations with clear error messages (e.g., devnet + Irys templates, move + fullstack-only flags)
- Auto-infer framework when template constrains to one option (e.g., `custom-indexer-template` → nextjs)

### New CLI interface

```bash
# Fully non-interactive (AI agent usage)
npx create-aptos-dapp my-app -p fullstack -t boilerplate-template -f vite -n testnet

# Move project (minimal flags)
npx create-aptos-dapp my-move-project -p move -n devnet

# Hybrid (prompt for missing values)
npx create-aptos-dapp --project-type fullstack --network testnet

# Discover valid options (for AI agents)
npx create-aptos-dapp --list

# Existing flows unchanged
npx create-aptos-dapp                    # Full interactive
npx create-aptos-dapp --example my-app   # Example generation
```

### Files changed

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/types.ts` | Added `CliFlags` interface and `PartialSelections` type |
| `src/utils/validateFlags.ts` | **New** — flag validation, normalization,
